clinicalStem: A 17-year-old male presents with a 2-year history of progressive difficulty walking and frequent falls. His parents report that he was a normal child who developed a clumsy gait around age 14. On examination he has dysarthria, bilateral lower-limb areflexia, extensor plantar responses, and reduced vibration and proprioception in both feet. Echocardiography reveals hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. His older sister had a similar presentation. Which diagnosis best fits this clinical picture?
options: Multiple sclerosis
Friedreich ataxia
Spinocerebellar ataxia type 3 (Machado-Joseph disease)
Ataxia-telangiectasia
Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease
explanation: Friedreich ataxia is an autosomal recessive condition caused by a GAA trinucleotide repeat expansion in the FXN gene. Classic features include onset before age 25, progressive cerebellar ataxia, absent lower-limb reflexes with extensor plantars (mixed UMN/LMN), posterior column sensory loss, dysarthria, and hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. The positive family history consistent with autosomal recessive inheritance and the cardiomyopathy strongly support this diagnosis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Multiple sclerosis typically presents in young adults with relapsing-remitting episodes, is rarely familial in this pattern, and does not cause hypertrophic cardiomyopathy or areflexia.
Friedreich ataxia is correct: onset in adolescence, areflexia with extensor plantars, posterior column loss, dysarthria, and hypertrophic cardiomyopathy are all hallmark features.
SCA3 is autosomal dominant and typically presents in adulthood; it does not cause cardiomyopathy and deep tendon reflexes are usually preserved or increased.
Ataxia-telangiectasia presents in early childhood (1-4 years), features oculocutaneous telangiectasias, and is associated with immune deficiency and elevated AFP — not hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.
Charcot-Marie-Tooth is a peripheral neuropathy causing distal weakness and wasting with foot deformity, but does not cause cerebellar ataxia or hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.
clinicalPearl: Friedreich ataxia = teenage-onset ataxia + absent ankle jerks + extensor plantars + hypertrophic cardiomyopathy; cardiomyopathy is the leading cause of death.
keyClue: Adolescent-onset progressive ataxia with areflexia, extensor plantars, posterior column loss, AND hypertrophic cardiomyopathy in a patient with an affected sibling.
commonExamTrap: Students may choose SCA (dominant) or MS, forgetting that the combination of absent reflexes WITH extensor plantars and cardiomyopathy in a teenager points squarely to Friedreich ataxia.
missedConcept: Mixed upper and lower motor neuron signs (absent reflexes + extensor plantars) alongside cerebellar ataxia and cardiomyopathy in adolescence is pathognomonic of Friedreich ataxia.

clinicalStem: A 58-year-old woman presents with a 3-week history of rapid-onset gait imbalance, dysarthria, and oscillopsia. She has no significant past medical history. On examination she has pan-cerebellar syndrome with opsoclonus-myoclonus and nystagmus. MRI brain with contrast is normal. Blood tests show normal B12, thyroid function, and glucose. She is a 40-pack-year smoker. Which investigation is most likely to establish the underlying aetiology?
options: Lumbar puncture and CSF oligoclonal bands
Anti-neuronal antibody panel (anti-Hu, anti-Yo, anti-Ri) and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis
Genetic testing for SCA1, SCA2, SCA3
Nerve conduction studies and EMG
Repeat MRI brain in 6 weeks
explanation: Sub-acute cerebellar ataxia with opsoclonus-myoclonus in a middle-aged heavy smoker should raise strong suspicion for paraneoplastic cerebellar degeneration. Anti-neuronal antibodies (anti-Hu for small cell lung cancer, anti-Yo for gynaecological/breast cancers, anti-Ri) combined with CT staging of chest/abdomen/pelvis is the most appropriate next step to identify the underlying malignancy and confirm the paraneoplastic aetiology.
wrongAnswerExplanations: CSF oligoclonal bands help diagnose MS, but the rapid sub-acute course, opsoclonus-myoclonus, and smoking history make paraneoplastic syndrome far more likely than MS.

Correct: paraneoplastic cerebellar degeneration is the leading diagnosis; anti-neuronal antibody panel combined with tumour search (CT CAP) is the investigation of choice in a smoker with sub-acute pan-cerebellar syndrome and opsoclonus.
Genetic testing for SCAs is appropriate for insidious hereditary ataxia, not for a rapidly evolving presentation in a 58-year-old smoker with opsoclonus-myoclonus.
NCS/EMG evaluates peripheral nerve disease; the examination findings point to a cerebellar/brainstem syndrome, not a peripheral neuropathy.
Repeat MRI in 6 weeks is inappropriate when a potentially treatable underlying cancer may be progressing; urgent investigation is needed.
clinicalPearl: Sub-acute cerebellar syndrome + opsoclonus-myoclonus in a smoker = paraneoplastic until proven otherwise; request anti-neuronal antibodies and a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis urgently.
keyClue: Rapidly progressive pan-cerebellar syndrome with opsoclonus-myoclonus in a 40-pack-year smoker with normal MRI brain.
commonExamTrap: Ordering repeat MRI or genetic testing instead of recognising the paraneoplastic pattern; normal MRI does not exclude paraneoplastic cerebellar degeneration.
missedConcept: Paraneoplastic cerebellar degeneration can precede tumour diagnosis by months; a normal MRI does not rule it out and anti-neuronal antibody testing with tumour screening is mandatory.

clinicalStem: A 22-year-old university student is brought to the emergency department by his housemates. He has had 6 hours of worsening headache, neck stiffness, and fever (39.8°C). His GCS is 13/15. On examination there is a non-blanching petechial rash over his trunk and lower limbs, positive Kernig and Brudzinski signs, and photophobia. Blood pressure is 88/52 mmHg. There is no papilloedema. What is the most appropriate IMMEDIATE management?
options: Urgent CT brain then lumbar puncture, then IV ceftriaxone 2g
IV ceftriaxone 2g immediately, then blood cultures, then CT brain and lumbar puncture if safe
Lumbar puncture immediately, then start IV antibiotics based on CSF results
IV dexamethasone alone pending microbiology results
Urgent MRI brain and neurosurgery referral
explanation: This patient has bacterial meningitis with meningococcaemia (non-blanching petechial rash, haemodynamic instability). In suspected bacterial meningitis, if there are no contraindications to LP (no papilloedema, no focal neurology, GCS >8), IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone 2g) plus dexamethasone should be given immediately after blood cultures — do not delay antibiotics for CT or LP. With haemodynamic compromise, resuscitation and antibiotics take absolute priority.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Delaying antibiotics for CT then LP is dangerous; in meningococcal disease with shock, every hour without antibiotics worsens mortality.

Correct: give IV ceftriaxone immediately (after blood cultures if possible), stabilise haemodynamics, then proceed to CT/LP. Dexamethasone should also be added. This follows UK/NICE meningitis guidelines.
LP first delays antibiotics; in a haemodynamically unstable patient with a non-blanching rash, LP should only follow antibiotic administration and haemodynamic stabilisation.
Dexamethasone alone without antibiotics would be dangerous and is never appropriate; dexamethasone is an adjunct to antibiotics, not a replacement.
MRI and neurosurgery are not the immediate priority; this is a medical emergency requiring immediate antibiotics and supportive resuscitation.
clinicalPearl: Never delay antibiotics for CT/LP in suspected bacterial meningitis — give IV ceftriaxone immediately after blood cultures; a non-blanching rash with meningism signals meningococcal septicaemia.
keyClue: Non-blanching petechial rash + meningism + fever + haemodynamic instability = meningococcal disease requiring immediate IV ceftriaxone.
commonExamTrap: Ordering CT brain first before antibiotics — guidelines mandate immediate antibiotics if no contraindications to LP exist; even if CT is needed first, antibiotics should not wait.
missedConcept: In bacterial meningitis, antibiotic delay beyond 1 hour significantly increases mortality; non-blanching rash indicates septicaemia and constitutes a medical emergency.
redFlagReason: Bacterial meningitis with meningococcal septicaemia and haemodynamic instability is immediately life-threatening; delayed antibiotic therapy increases mortality dramatically.

clinicalStem: A 45-year-old woman presents with 4 days of fever (38.5°C), confusion, and two witnessed tonic-clonic seizures. Her husband reports personality changes and bizarre behaviour over the past week. On mental state examination she is disoriented, agitated, and has auditory hallucinations. Neurological examination reveals mild left-sided facial droop. MRI brain shows T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in the right temporal lobe and insular cortex. CSF shows 120 lymphocytes, protein 0.9 g/L, normal glucose. Which is the most important immediate treatment?
options: IV aciclovir 10 mg/kg every 8 hours
IV ceftriaxone 2g twice daily
Oral valaciclovir 1g three times daily
IV methylprednisolone 1g daily
Broad-spectrum antifungals pending India ink stain
explanation: This presentation is classic for herpes simplex encephalitis (HSE): fever, personality change, temporal lobe signs, seizures, CSF lymphocytosis, and MRI hyperintensity in the temporal lobe/insula. HSE is the most common cause of fatal sporadic encephalitis and must be treated immediately with IV aciclovir 10 mg/kg every 8 hours. Treatment should not await PCR confirmation because the condition is rapidly fatal without antiviral therapy.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: IV aciclovir 10 mg/kg TDS is the treatment of choice for HSE; temporal lobe involvement with behavioural change, seizures, and lymphocytic CSF pleocytosis is classic for HSV-1 encephalitis.
IV ceftriaxone covers bacterial meningitis; the lymphocytic CSF, temporal MRI changes, and temporal lobe symptoms point to viral (HSV) encephalitis, not bacterial meningitis.
Oral valaciclovir does not achieve adequate CNS concentrations for encephalitis; IV formulation is mandatory for HSE.
Steroids alone without antivirals would be harmful; while some clinicians add dexamethasone adjunctively, IV aciclovir is the essential immediate treatment.
Antifungal therapy targets cryptococcal or fungal meningitis; CSF findings and MRI temporal involvement are not consistent with fungal aetiology in an immunocompetent patient.
clinicalPearl: Temporal lobe MRI changes + behavioural disturbance + fever + seizures = herpes simplex encephalitis; start IV aciclovir 10 mg/kg TDS immediately — do not wait for HSV PCR.
keyClue: T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in the right temporal lobe and insula + fever + behavioural change + seizures = HSV encephalitis.
commonExamTrap: Treating with antibiotics alone and missing the antiviral; oral aciclovir is insufficient — IV route is mandatory for encephalitis.
missedConcept: HSV encephalitis has a predilection for the temporal lobes and limbic system, causing personality change, psychiatric features, and seizures; without IV aciclovir, mortality approaches 70%.
redFlagReason: Untreated HSV encephalitis has mortality approaching 70%; immediate IV aciclovir is life-saving and must not be delayed pending PCR confirmation.

clinicalStem: A 19-year-old male is brought to the emergency department after being struck on the left side of his head by a cricket ball. He briefly lost consciousness for 30 seconds, then appeared fully alert and chatted with bystanders. Two hours later he develops a severe headache, becomes drowsy, and his GCS drops from 15 to 10. His left pupil is now 6mm and unreactive. CT head shows a biconvex hyperdense lesion over the left temporal region. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: IV mannitol and observe in the neurology ward
Urgent neurosurgical referral for craniotomy and evacuation of haematoma
IV dexamethasone and repeat CT in 4 hours
Intubation, ventilation to PCO2 of 4.5 kPa, and MRI brain
IV ceftriaxone and aciclovir to cover infective causes
explanation: This is a classic extradural haematoma (EDH): trauma to the temporal region (middle meningeal artery territory), brief initial LOC, lucid interval, then rapid neurological deterioration with a fixed dilated pupil (uncal herniation). A biconvex hyperdense lesion on CT confirms the diagnosis. EDH is a neurosurgical emergency requiring immediate craniotomy and clot evacuation; delay can result in brainstem herniation and death.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Mannitol may be used as a temporising measure to reduce ICP, but is not definitive treatment; the patient requires urgent surgical evacuation and should be referred immediately.

Correct: urgent neurosurgical craniotomy and evacuation is the definitive treatment for EDH with neurological deterioration and signs of uncal herniation (fixed dilated pupil); every minute of delay worsens outcome.
Dexamethasone does not treat EDH and delaying surgery with repeat imaging in an already herniating patient is dangerous.
Hyperventilation to lower PCO2 is a temporising measure for raised ICP but definitive surgery must not be delayed; MRI is contraindicated in an unstable deteriorating patient.
Antibiotics and antivirals are not indicated; this is a traumatic surgical emergency, not an infective one.
clinicalPearl: Lucid interval after head trauma then rapid deterioration + fixed dilated pupil + biconvex CT lesion = extradural haematoma; this is a neurosurgical emergency — operate immediately.
keyClue: Lucid interval followed by GCS deterioration and ipsilateral fixed dilated pupil after temporal head trauma = extradural haematoma with uncal herniation.
commonExamTrap: Choosing medical management (mannitol, dexamethasone) instead of urgent surgery; while temporising measures exist, definitive treatment is craniotomy.
missedConcept: The lucid interval is the pathognomonic feature of EDH — arterial bleeding (middle meningeal artery) leads to progressive haematoma expansion after a brief recovery period.
redFlagReason: Extradural haematoma with uncal herniation (fixed dilated pupil, GCS deterioration) is immediately life-threatening; surgical delay causes irreversible brainstem damage and death.

clinicalStem: A 78-year-old woman is brought to clinic by her daughter who is concerned about progressive confusion over the past 6 weeks. She lives alone and is known to take warfarin for atrial fibrillation. She denies any head injury but the daughter recalls that she had a minor fall about 8 weeks ago. On examination her GCS is 14/15, there is mild left-sided upper limb weakness, and her INR today is 3.8. MMSE is 18/30 — a decline from 26/30 six months ago. CT head is requested. What is the most likely finding?
options: Acute extradural haematoma
Hypertensive intracerebral haemorrhage in the right basal ganglia
Bilateral hypodense crescent-shaped collections over both cerebral convexities
Multiple ring-enhancing lesions consistent with cerebral metastases
Diffuse cortical atrophy consistent with Alzheimer disease
explanation: This presentation is classic for chronic subdural haematoma (CSDH): elderly patient on anticoagulation, minor head trauma 8 weeks prior (often forgotten), progressive cognitive decline, mild focal neurology, and supratherapeutic INR. Chronic SDH appears on CT as bilateral (or unilateral) hypodense or isodense crescent-shaped collections over the cerebral convexities — the crescent shape distinguishes it from the biconvex EDH.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Extradural haematoma is biconvex, hyperdense, and follows acute high-velocity trauma — not an 8-week insidious course.

Hypertensive ICH typically affects basal ganglia/thalamus/posterior fossa acutely in a patient with poorly controlled hypertension; it does not cause a 6-week progressive course with a crescent-shaped collection.

Correct: chronic SDH (hypodense crescent-shaped collections) is the classic diagnosis in an elderly anticoagulated patient with minor trauma weeks prior and progressive cognitive and focal neurological decline.
Ring-enhancing lesions suggest cerebral metastases or abscesses — no evidence of malignancy history and the temporal relationship to minor trauma makes CSDH far more likely.

Diffuse cortical atrophy explains a chronic dementia syndrome but not the focal neurology, the temporal relationship to trauma, or the anticoagulation; MMSE decline over only 6 months is too rapid for typical Alzheimer progression.
clinicalPearl: Elderly patient + anticoagulation + minor fall weeks ago + progressive cognitive decline + subtle focal neurology = chronic subdural haematoma until proven otherwise.
keyClue: Minor fall 8 weeks ago, warfarin with elevated INR, progressive cognitive and neurological decline over 6 weeks in an elderly patient.
commonExamTrap: Attributing cognitive decline solely to dementia without considering reversible structural causes; chronic SDH is a treatable cause of cognitive decline in elderly anticoagulated patients.
missedConcept: Chronic SDH can present weeks after minor (or forgotten) head trauma, especially in elderly anticoagulated patients; CT shows hypodense crescent-shaped collections not biconvex hyperdense collections.

clinicalStem: A 32-year-old man with schizophrenia is admitted from the community. His haloperidol dose was doubled 5 days ago by his outpatient team. He presents with temperature 40.2°C, blood pressure 160/100 mmHg, heart rate 128 bpm, generalised lead-pipe rigidity, diaphoresis, and a fluctuating conscious level (GCS 11/15). CK is 18,400 IU/L. Urine dipstick shows 3+ blood (no RBCs on microscopy). What is the most important initial intervention?
options: Add a second antipsychotic and admit to psychiatry ward
Start IV dantrolene and bromocriptine, immediately stop haloperidol, and transfer to ICU
Increase haloperidol to control agitation and monitor closely
Give IM procyclidine for extrapyramidal side effects
Initiate rapid oral rehydration and paracetamol for fever
explanation: This is neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS): hyperthermia, lead-pipe rigidity, autonomic instability, altered consciousness, and markedly elevated CK after a dose increase of a dopamine-blocking agent. Myoglobinuria (positive blood dipstick with no RBCs) indicates rhabdomyolysis, risking acute kidney injury. Management requires immediate cessation of the offending antipsychotic, ICU transfer, aggressive IV hydration, dantrolene (muscle relaxant) and bromocriptine (dopamine agonist), and supportive care.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Adding another antipsychotic would worsen NMS by further dopamine blockade; this patient needs antipsychotic cessation, not continuation or addition.

Correct: immediately stop haloperidol, start IV dantrolene (reduces rigidity and hyperthermia) and bromocriptine (restores dopaminergic tone), aggressive IV fluids for rhabdomyolysis/AKI prevention, and ICU-level supportive care.
Increasing haloperidol would be catastrophically dangerous — NMS is caused by dopamine blockade and any further dopaminergic suppression can be fatal.
Procyclidine is used for acute dystonia and Parkinsonism, not NMS; anticholinergic agents do not address the core pathophysiology and will not reverse rigidity or hyperthermia in NMS.
Oral rehydration and paracetamol are entirely insufficient for a patient with 40°C hyperthermia, CK of 18,400, rhabdomyolysis, and altered consciousness; this patient requires ICU care.
clinicalPearl: NMS triad: hyperthermia + lead-pipe rigidity + autonomic instability after antipsychotic use; markedly elevated CK signals rhabdomyolysis — stop the drug immediately and give dantrolene + bromocriptine in ICU.
keyClue: Recent haloperidol dose increase + temperature 40.2°C + lead-pipe rigidity + CK 18,400 + myoglobinuria = neuroleptic malignant syndrome.
commonExamTrap: Confusing NMS with serotonin syndrome (both cause hyperthermia); lead-pipe rigidity and markedly elevated CK with antipsychotic exposure is NMS; serotonin syndrome features clonus and hyperreflexia.
missedConcept: NMS is a life-threatening dopamine blockade reaction; the antipsychotic must be stopped immediately — continuing or adding antipsychotics is fatal.
redFlagReason: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome with rhabdomyolysis and altered consciousness is a medical emergency with significant mortality if the causative drug is not stopped immediately and ICU care initiated.

clinicalStem: A 52-year-old woman with bipolar affective disorder maintained on lithium 800 mg daily presents to the emergency department. She was recently prescribed ibuprofen by her GP for knee pain. She complains of 3 days of coarse tremor, vomiting, blurred vision, and unsteady gait. On examination there is nystagmus, dysarthria, hyperreflexia, and confusion. Her serum lithium level is 2.8 mmol/L (therapeutic range 0.6–1.0 mmol/L). What is the most appropriate management?
options: Withhold lithium for 24 hours and recheck level
Stop lithium, IV fluid resuscitation, and urgent renal replacement therapy (haemodialysis)
Reduce lithium dose by half and add sodium valproate
Give activated charcoal and observe for 4 hours
Start IV sodium bicarbonate to alkalinise the urine
explanation: This patient has severe lithium toxicity (level 2.8 mmol/L with neurological features including confusion, nystagmus, dysarthria, hyperreflexia). NSAID use (ibuprofen) caused lithium retention by reducing renal prostaglandin synthesis. Severe toxicity with neurological features and a level >2.5 mmol/L is an indication for haemodialysis to remove lithium rapidly and prevent irreversible cerebellar damage (SILENT syndrome). IV normal saline resuscitation should run alongside.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Simply withholding lithium is inadequate for a level of 2.8 mmol/L with confusion and neurological signs; haemodialysis is required at this severity.

Correct: stop lithium, administer IV 0.9% saline for volume and renal support, and arrange urgent haemodialysis — this is the standard of care for severe lithium toxicity (level >2.5 mmol/L) with neurological compromise.
Dose reduction and adding valproate does not address the acute toxicity; the lithium level needs to be actively removed.
Activated charcoal does not bind lithium (lithium is an ion) and is therefore ineffective; it has no role in lithium toxicity management.
Alkalinisation of urine promotes reabsorption of lithium (lithium excretion is favoured by normal saline, not alkalinisation); sodium bicarbonate is not recommended and would worsen toxicity.
clinicalPearl: NSAIDs reduce renal lithium excretion and precipitate toxicity; a lithium level >2.5 mmol/L with neurological features mandates haemodialysis — irreversible cerebellar damage (SILENT syndrome) can occur if treatment is delayed.
keyClue: Recent NSAID (ibuprofen) addition to lithium regimen + lithium level 2.8 mmol/L + confusion, dysarthria, nystagmus, hyperreflexia.
commonExamTrap: Thinking activated charcoal works for all overdoses — lithium is not absorbed by charcoal; and alkalinisation enhances lithium REABSORPTION, which is the opposite of what is needed.
missedConcept: Lithium has a narrow therapeutic window; NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors, and dehydration all cause lithium retention and toxicity; haemodialysis is required for severe toxicity.

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old woman with depression is taking fluoxetine 40 mg daily. Her GP recently added tramadol 50 mg QDS for lower back pain. She presents to the emergency department with agitation, confusion, temperature 38.8°C, blood pressure 155/95 mmHg, heart rate 118 bpm, and diaphoresis. On examination she has bilateral clonus, hyperreflexia, and bilateral inducible ocular clonus. Which mechanism best explains her presentation?
options: Dopamine receptor blockade by fluoxetine causing neuroleptic malignant syndrome
Excess serotonergic activity due to combined SSRI and opioid serotonin reuptake inhibition
Cholinergic toxidrome from tramadol-fluoxetine interaction
Noradrenergic crisis secondary to SSRI discontinuation syndrome
Mitochondrial uncoupling causing thermogenesis independent of serotonin
explanation: This is serotonin syndrome caused by the combination of fluoxetine (SSRI — blocks 5-HT reuptake) and tramadol (weak 5-HT reuptake inhibitor AND mu-opioid agonist). Excess synaptic serotonin produces the clinical triad: mental status changes, autonomic instability, and neuromuscular abnormalities (clonus is the most specific feature). The combination of two serotonergic agents precipitated the syndrome.
wrongAnswerExplanations: NMS results from dopamine receptor blockade (antipsychotics); it features lead-pipe rigidity and markedly elevated CK — not clonus and hyperreflexia; fluoxetine is not a dopamine blocker.

Correct: tramadol inhibits serotonin reuptake (like an SSRI) and when combined with fluoxetine (a potent SSRI), the additive serotonergic effect causes serotonin syndrome characterised by clonus, hyperreflexia, agitation, and autonomic instability.
Cholinergic toxidrome presents with SLUDGE (salivation, lacrimation, urination, defaecation, GI distress, emesis) and miosis — the features here are not consistent.
SSRI discontinuation syndrome occurs when stopping an SSRI, not when adding a second serotonergic agent; it presents with flu-like symptoms and electric-shock sensations, not clonus.
Mitochondrial uncoupling is the mechanism for some thermogenic drugs (e.g. DNP poisoning); this is not relevant to the SSRI-tramadol interaction.
clinicalPearl: Tramadol has serotonin reuptake inhibitor activity — combining it with any SSRI/SNRI risks serotonin syndrome; clonus (ocular, ankle) is the most specific clinical sign.
keyClue: Fluoxetine + tramadol combination + bilateral clonus + hyperreflexia + agitation + hyperthermia = serotonin syndrome.
commonExamTrap: Confusing serotonin syndrome with NMS; clonus/hyperreflexia distinguishes serotonin syndrome from NMS (lead-pipe rigidity); tramadol is often forgotten as a serotonergic drug.
missedConcept: Tramadol is a dual-mechanism opioid with significant serotonin reuptake inhibition; its combination with SSRIs is a common iatrogenic cause of serotonin syndrome.

clinicalStem: A 46-year-old homeless man is admitted to a general ward. He last drank alcohol 48 hours ago (approximately 20 units per day for 10 years). On admission he was tremulous; now he is acutely confused, agitated, and his observations show temperature 38.1°C, HR 132 bpm, BP 168/100 mmHg, and diaphoresis. He is having visual hallucinations of insects on the walls. On neurological examination there are coarse tremors and he is hyperreflexic. Which is the most appropriate immediate pharmacological management?
options: IV haloperidol 5 mg to control hallucinations and agitation
IV lorazepam with a symptom-triggered CIWA-Ar protocol, thiamine before glucose
Oral diazepam 20 mg stat as an outpatient regimen
IM olanzapine to control agitation and oral thiamine
IV sodium valproate loading dose for seizure prevention
explanation: This patient has delirium tremens (DT): confusion, autonomic hyperactivity, hallucinations at 48 hours after cessation in a heavy long-term drinker. Benzodiazepines are the treatment of choice — IV lorazepam or diazepam titrated via CIWA-Ar scoring. Parenteral thiamine (Pabrinex) MUST be given BEFORE any glucose-containing fluids to prevent precipitating Wernicke encephalopathy. DT carries up to 5% mortality without treatment and requires HDU/ICU level care.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Haloperidol lowers the seizure threshold and does not treat the underlying GABA-ergic deficit of alcohol withdrawal; antipsychotics should not be used as monotherapy for DT.
Correct: IV benzodiazepines (lorazepam) titrated by CIWA-Ar are first-line; IV thiamine (Pabrinex) must precede any dextrose to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy; this patient requires HDU escalation.
Oral diazepam is appropriate for mild-moderate withdrawal in community patients who can self-medicate; DT with autonomic crisis requires IV therapy in a monitored setting.
Olanzapine reduces the seizure threshold and is not adequate monotherapy for delirium tremens; oral thiamine (oral thiamine has poor bioavailability compared to IV Pabrinex) is also insufficient.
Sodium valproate is not first-line for DT management; benzodiazepines are preferred as they address both seizure risk and autonomic instability.
clinicalPearl: DT = benzodiazepines first-line (CIWA-Ar guided); always give IV Pabrinex BEFORE dextrose to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy — this sequence saves lives.
keyClue: 48 hours post last drink in a heavy long-term drinker + confusion + visual hallucinations + autonomic hyperactivity = delirium tremens.
commonExamTrap: Using antipsychotics (haloperidol) as the primary agent for DT hallucinations — antipsychotics lower seizure threshold; benzodiazepines treat both the hallucinations and seizure risk.
missedConcept: Thiamine must always precede glucose in alcohol-dependent patients to prevent precipitating Wernicke encephalopathy; benzodiazepines, not antipsychotics, are first-line for alcohol withdrawal delirium.
redFlagReason: Delirium tremens carries up to 5% mortality and risk of withdrawal seizures; inadequate benzodiazepine treatment and omission of parenteral thiamine are immediately dangerous clinical errors.

clinicalStem: Paramedics bring a 35-year-old male found unconscious in a public toilet with a syringe beside him. On examination: GCS 6/15, respiratory rate 4 breaths/min, SpO2 78% on air, pinpoint pupils bilaterally, clammy skin, and bradycardia (HR 44 bpm). BP is 84/50 mmHg. There are track marks on both arms. Which immediate action is most appropriate?
options: CT head to exclude intracranial haemorrhage before any intervention
Airway management and IV naloxone 400 micrograms, repeat as needed
IM diazepam for suspected benzodiazepine co-ingestion
IV glucose 50 mL of 50% dextrose to exclude hypoglycaemia
Oral activated charcoal via nasogastric tube
explanation: This patient presents with the classic opioid toxidrome: reduced GCS, respiratory depression (RR 4), pinpoint pupils, hypotension, and bradycardia in a known IV drug user. Immediate management follows ABCDE: secure airway, assist ventilation with high-flow oxygen, and administer IV naloxone 400 micrograms (may repeat up to 10 mg) to reverse opioid-induced respiratory depression. Naloxone has a shorter half-life than most opioids and repeat doses or an infusion may be needed.
wrongAnswerExplanations: CT head is not the immediate priority in a patient with a classic opioid toxidrome; airway protection and naloxone must come first — delaying reversal risks respiratory arrest and death.
Correct: the opioid toxidrome (pinpoint pupils, respiratory depression, coma, track marks) requires immediate airway management and IV naloxone; this is the life-saving intervention.
IM diazepam would worsen respiratory depression by adding CNS depression on top of opioids; the clinical picture is not consistent with benzodiazepine overdose as the primary cause.
While hypoglycaemia should be excluded, IV glucose is not the immediate priority in a patient with a clear opioid toxidrome and SpO2 of 78%; airway and naloxone take precedence.
Activated charcoal via NGT is contraindicated in a patient with GCS 6 (aspiration risk) and would be ineffective for IV opioid use; it has no role here.
clinicalPearl: Opioid toxidrome = coma + pinpoint pupils + respiratory depression; give naloxone immediately — this is the antidote; expect reversal of miosis and improved respiratory rate within minutes.
keyClue: Pinpoint pupils + respiratory rate 4 + GCS 6 + track marks = opioid overdose; naloxone is the immediate antidote.
commonExamTrap: Performing CT head or checking glucose before giving naloxone — in a clear opioid toxidrome, airway management and naloxone must not be delayed.
missedConcept: Naloxone has a shorter half-life than heroin and other opioids; a single dose may not suffice — repeated dosing or an infusion is often required to maintain reversal.
redFlagReason: Opioid-induced respiratory depression (RR 4, SpO2 78%) is an immediately life-threatening emergency; delayed naloxone administration leads to respiratory arrest and brain injury.

clinicalStem: A 54-year-old woman with known alcohol dependence is admitted for elective hernia repair. She is nutritionally depleted (BMI 17.2). On day 2 post-operatively she develops acute confusion, bilateral lateral gaze palsy (inability to abduct both eyes), and a broad-based ataxic gait. Her blood glucose is 9.2 mmol/L and she was given 5% dextrose as her maintenance fluid since admission. Temperature is 37.2°C and CT head is normal. What is the most likely diagnosis and what is the precipitating factor for this presentation?
options: Alcohol withdrawal delirium precipitated by abrupt cessation after surgery
Wernicke encephalopathy precipitated by glucose administration in a thiamine-deficient patient
Post-operative delirium due to anaesthetic agents and opioid analgesia
Acute ischaemic stroke affecting the dorsal midbrain and vermis
Hyponatraemia causing cerebral oedema and cranial nerve palsies
explanation: The classic triad of Wernicke encephalopathy is confusion + ophthalmoplegia (classically lateral gaze palsy/nystagmus) + ataxia. It is caused by thiamine deficiency and is precipitated in alcohol-dependent patients by glucose loading without prior thiamine supplementation — glucose metabolism consumes thiamine, precipitating acute Wernicke encephalopathy. The dextrose infusion on a background of nutritional depletion is the critical precipitating factor here.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Alcohol withdrawal delirium presents with autonomic hyperactivity, tremors, and hallucinations typically 24-72 hours post cessation; it does not cause the specific ophthalmoplegia and ataxia triad without other features.

Correct: the classic Wernicke triad (confusion + lateral gaze palsy + ataxia) in a thiamine-depleted alcohol-dependent patient precipitated by dextrose infusion is diagnostic of Wernicke encephalopathy; immediate IV Pabrinex is required.
Post-operative delirium is common but does not specifically cause bilateral lateral gaze palsy and ataxia; the specific ophthalmoplegia points to a structural thiamine-deficient lesion.

Acute ischaemic stroke would be visible on diffusion MRI (CT is insensitive in posterior fossa); the temporal relationship to glucose infusion and the clinical triad make Wernicke encephalopathy far more likely.
Hyponatraemia causes generalised confusion and seizures but not the specific ophthalmoplegia-ataxia-confusion triad; electrolytes are not reported as abnormal here.
clinicalPearl: Never give glucose to alcohol-dependent or malnourished patients without giving IV thiamine (Pabrinex) first — glucose infusion precipitates Wernicke encephalopathy in thiamine-depleted patients.
keyClue: Confusion + bilateral lateral gaze palsy + ataxia + thiamine-depleted alcohol-dependent patient who received dextrose infusion = Wernicke encephalopathy.
commonExamTrap: Attributing the confusion to post-operative delirium or alcohol withdrawal alone without recognising the specific ophthalmoplegia-ataxia triad that defines Wernicke encephalopathy.
missedConcept: Wernicke encephalopathy requires the triad of confusion, ophthalmoplegia, and ataxia; it is precipitated by glucose without thiamine in malnourished patients — thiamine must always precede glucose.
